ICtoolkit 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Compute EBIC for model objects

Source: R/compute_ebic.R
compute_ebic.Rd

Computes the Extended Bayesian Information Criterion (EBIC) of Chen & Chen (2008). EBIC adds a sparsity-encouraging penalty to BIC:

compute_ebic(fit, P = NULL, gamma = "ebic", ...)

Arguments

fit

A fitted model object of class lm, glm, glmnet, or ncvreg.

P

Integer. Total number of candidate predictors (excluding the intercept) considered during model selection. If NULL (the default), inferred from fit (see Inferring P section).

gamma

Controls the sparsity tuning parameter. One of:

"ebic" (default)

The data-adaptive rule of Chen & Chen (2008): \(\gamma = \log(P/\sqrt{n}) / \log P \cdot \mathbf{1}(P \ge \sqrt{n})\).

A numeric scalar

Fixed gamma value, e.g. gamma = 0.5.

A function f(P, k, n)

Custom rule; advanced use only.

...

Currently unused; reserved for future use.

Value

A named numeric scalar (lm/glm) or vector (glmnet/ncvreg) with the following attributes:

fit_class

Character. The primary class of fit.

k

Integer scalar or vector. Number of estimated parameters (including intercept).

gamma

Numeric scalar or vector. The computed gamma value(s).

P

Integer. The value of P used.

criterion

"EBIC".

Details

$$\mathrm{EBIC} = \mathrm{BIC} + 2\,\gamma\,\log\binom{P}{k}$$

where P is the total number of candidate predictors, k is the number of selected predictors (nonzero coefficients, excluding the intercept), and gamma is a tuning parameter.

For glmnet and ncvreg, EBIC is computed at each point on the regularization path (one value per lambda).

Inferring P

If P is not supplied, it is inferred from the model object:

glmnet

nrow(fit$beta) — the number of columns in the original design matrix X.

ncvreg

nrow(fit$beta) - 1 — excluding the intercept row.

lm / glm

length(coef(fit)) - 1 — the number of terms in the fitted model. This is correct only when the fitted model contains all candidate predictors (e.g. the full model); if the model is a subset, you should supply P explicitly.

References

Chen, J., & Chen, Z. (2008). Extended Bayesian information criteria for model selection with large model spaces. Biometrika, 95(3), 759–771.

See also

compute_bic(), compute_rbic(), ic_step()

Examples

set.seed(1)
X <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 20), 100, 20)
y <- X[, 1:3] %*% c(2, -1, 0.5) + rnorm(100)
fit <- lm(y ~ X)

compute_ebic(fit, P = 20)               # explicit P
#> [1] 378.9493
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0.2313782
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
compute_ebic(fit)                        # infers P = 20 from model
#> [1] 378.9493
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0.2313782
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
compute_ebic(fit, P = 20, gamma = 0.5)  # fixed gamma
#> [1] 378.9493
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0.5
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
compute_ebic(fit, P = 20, gamma = 0)    # gamma = 0 recovers BIC
#> [1] 378.9493
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "EBIC"
#> attr(,"gamma")
#> [1] 0
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
